Costa Rica Histórica · Fondo Fidel Tristán Fernández

The Garden of the Observatory

Three photographs of the Observatorio Meteorológico Nacional, 1920–1930
Archivo Nacional de Costa Rica · Fototeca · Document 166 · Ref. CR-AN-AH-FO-079098/079099/079100

A single photographic session, or perhaps a brief succession of days at the same site, produced these three images, which the Archivo Nacional keeps grouped under a single document of the Fondo Fidel Tristán. Together they form a small diptych — a triptych, really — of the work and scientific life at the Observatorio Meteorológico Nacional around 1920–1930: the astronomical observation instrument, the physics cabinet with its discharge tube, and, between the two, a moment of camaraderie with glasses raised in the same garden.

79098The Telescope
→
79099The Toast
→
79100The Cabinet
CR-AN-AH-FO-079098 · Documento 166 I · Instrument

The Observatory Garden

Three men of science and a refracting telescope

The photograph documents an observation session or instrument demonstration: a long-tubed refracting telescope, mounted on a cast-iron column with an equatorial head, aimed upward at a steep angle. Montandón, seated at center, holds the tube with both hands while directing the eyepiece; J.F. Tristán, at left, watches in profile, seated on a wooden bench identical to his companion's. At right, standing in formal street dress — waistcoat, bow tie, neatly trimmed mustache — Pedro Reitz stands beside a large-format plate camera on a wooden tripod, with the characteristic dark cloth draped over its side.

On the concrete pedestal that runs across the foreground, the station's support instruments are visible: what appears to be a theodolite or transit instrument on its own tripod, a chronometer case, stacked books, and what could be a pith helmet of the field-campaign type. In the background, an unrendered brick wall — probably part of the observatory or institute building itself — and the vegetation of an urban San José garden of the period: banana plants, citrus trees, and ornamental shrubs.

J. F. Tristán

Naturalist · Educator

José Fidel Tristán Fernández (1874–1932), San José. Costa Rica's first entomologist, assistant and later curator at the Museo Nacional, professor and director of the Liceo de Costa Rica, pioneer of radiotelegraphy and of infrared and ultraviolet light photography in the country.

Montandón

Identity unconfirmed

Appears only by surname in the original inscription. No biography or institutional record has yet been located that identifies him with certainty. This remains an open research thread.

Pedro Reitz

Engineer

Documented in the Archivo Nacional as an engineer, co-author — together with surveying engineer Pedro Nolasco Gutiérrez — of the barometric pressure summaries of the Instituto Físico-Geográfico (1889–1897).

On Montandón: the surname points to a family of Swiss-French origin with a documented presence in Latin American scientific and educational circles of the era, but I have not yet been able to confirm a specific identification within Costa Rica's teaching or technical staff of the period.

CR-AN-AH-FO-079099 · Documento 166 II · Camaraderie

A Toast in the Garden

A raised glass, the same garden

If the first photograph documents the instrument, this one documents the sociability that surrounded it. On a table covered with a dark cloth, five tall-stemmed liqueur glasses are lined up alongside a bottle and a wide glass, all empty or half-poured. A standing man, wearing a straw boater hat, dark waistcoat, and bow tie, smiles openly at the camera while raising in his left hand a cylindrical, elongated object — possibly another bottle, or perhaps a piece of laboratory glassware similar to that in the cabinet photographed in the third plate — and holding a glass in his right. To his left, a second man, in a light waistcoat and shirtsleeves, looks directly at the lens with a calm expression; to the right, a third man converses in profile with a fourth, partially visible, dressed similarly, in what looks like a side conversation within the same gathering.

The image is considerably more deteriorated than its companions — creases, tears, and damp stains cross the surface of the original photograph — suggesting it circulated or was kept differently from the other two, perhaps as a personal keepsake rather than an institutional record. The background, with its dense vegetation, corresponds to the same garden seen in the telescope photograph.

On the occasion: there is no textual indication of what was being celebrated — it could be a birthday, a visit, the close of an observation campaign, or simply the evening custom of colleagues who shared years of work together at the same site. The Archivo Nacional's record catalogs it simply as taken "in the garden of the place," reinforcing the reading that this is the same Observatorio, captured at another moment of the same day or a nearby one.

J. F. Tristán

Present in both photographs

His presence in this image, alongside the telescope photograph, confirms that both belong to the same sphere of activity and likely the same day at the Observatorio.

Enrique Iglesias · Enrique Fernández

Identities unconfirmed

Both names are too common in the Costa Rican record of the period to identify with certainty without further documentary context. No biographical reference has yet been found linking them to the Instituto Físico-Geográfico, the Liceo de Costa Rica, or Tristán's circle beyond this photograph.

CR-AN-AH-FO-079100 · Documento 166 III · Cabinet

The Cabinet of Instruments

A discharge tube and the physics cabinet

The third photograph leaves the garden to enter the interior of the building: a corner of a cabinet or technical workshop, photographed with no people present, as a pure inventory of the available instruments. At center, mounted horizontally on a bench stand with a clamp and vertical rod, a gas discharge tube of the Crookes type is visible — its electrical terminals clearly visible at both ends — connected by a tangle of wiring to a power source on a small white block. Beside the tube, a sheet of card or paper folded at an angle serves as a screen, probably to observe fluorescence or cast a shadow during the demonstration.

On the two wooden shelves that dominate the right half of the image, the rest of the cabinet can now be seen clearly: large glass cylinders and jars, a mortar with its pestle, several reagent bottles with labels illegible at this distance, and, at the end of the upper shelf, what looks like a small sprig or plant cutting — an almost domestic detail amid the scientific instruments. Below, hanging on the wall, a coiled hose or rubber tube, a wheel or disc that could be a friction or hand-cranked induction generator, a metal ladle or strainer, and several pointed objects — tongs or glass rods — standing in a container. The assemblage is unmistakably a space for experimental physics and chemistry, not for fieldwork.

On the discharge tube: Crookes tubes — direct forerunners of the X-ray tube — were, by the 1920s, standard teaching equipment in any serious physics cabinet: they served to demonstrate the nature of cathode rays, the fluorescence induced by electrical discharge in rarefied gas, and in some cases to produce rudimentary radiographs. Given Tristán's documented interest in ultraviolet and infrared light photography — and his role as a pioneer of radiotelegraphy in Costa Rica — it would not be surprising if this instrument belonged to his own program of experimentation, though the photograph itself does not confirm this explicitly.
Physics Cabinet Crookes Tube Laboratory Instruments No Human Figures

The Institute Behind the Garden

The Observatorio that serves as the setting for these three photographs was not an isolated institution, but one of the three original branches of the Instituto Físico-Geográfico Nacional, founded by decree on 11 June 1889 under the direction of the Swiss naturalist Henri Pittier. The founding decree explicitly joined meteorological observation with the country's cartographic survey and with the nascent Museo Nacional — a single project of the State to know, measure, and represent Costa Rican territory.

Observatorio Meteorológico + Servicio Geográfico + Museo Nacional = Instituto Físico-Geográfico (1889)

Tristán, Reitz, Pittier, Pedro Nolasco Gutiérrez, Paul Biolley, Gustavo Michaud — all belong to this same institutional network, generally interwoven with the Liceo de Costa Rica as a teaching seedbed. The Observatorio Meteorológico Nacional was, in that sense, both a practical measuring station (pressure, temperature, seismicity, astronomical observation) and a social space and a physics demonstration cabinet — exactly the three registers these photographs document.

Three Registers of a Single Place

Seen together, the three photographs form something more than three loose images: they document the three simultaneous registers in which a Costa Rican scientific institution of the 1920s operated. There is the observation instrument — the telescope in the garden, handled with the seriousness of men who know they are being photographed for posterity; there is the social life that sustained institutional work — colleagues sharing a drink in the same garden, in a negative that has suffered more with the passage of time, as befits perhaps a more intimate keepsake than an official one; and there is the interior cabinet, stripped of human presence, where science was practiced with discharge tubes, reagents, and the silent discipline of a workshop.

Tristán appears in two of the three — the thread that explicitly links them — while Montandón, Reitz, Enrique Iglesias, and Enrique Fernández pass through the frame leaving, for now, little more than their names. This is exactly the kind of document that rewards patience: every name without a confirmed biography is an invitation to keep revisiting the Tristán fondo and the records of the former Instituto Físico-Geográfico.

Costa Rica Histórica — Archival Photography Catalogue
Source: Archivo Nacional de Costa Rica, Fondo Fidel Tristán Fernández
